moment_text: That's a great question. I honestly, I don't know if there was a singular moment. I have been very fortunate to have exposure to different populations, different groups. Um, very early in my life, the first few years of my life, my mother and I were, were homeless. And through that experience, my mom actually created a nonprofit with other homeless individuals, uh, an organization that still operates in Philadelphia today called Dignity Housing. Uh, and so during that time I essentially watched my mom build something from scratch and Dignity Housing. Essentially supported mothers and families who were unhoused or homeless, but not just providing them housing, but education and were advocates and, you know, helping them even just understand different systems. And so during, during those years, you know, I was. At the shelters with my mom, I'm witnessing the, the trauma that children had to experience when they were unhoused. And so even then as a child, I always knew I wanted to work with children. I wasn't sure in what capacity, but I watched and, you know, even certainly had my, my own experiences where I'm like, kids need help. They, they need someone that they can trust. And I fortunately had a wonderful family and a wonderful grandmother who. Literally never said no to me, gave me everything I could possibly want. And so I, I also recognized the benefit of having that unconditional love and having that support while also witnessing a lot of the children and families that my mothers, my mother worked with, did not have that support.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: Contains a barrier signal (lack_of_support) and describes a formative lived experience within a community system interaction.

moment_text: So, you know, going into college, I knew I wanted to do something with kids. I wasn't sure. I, I majored in, um, psychology and nutrition. I was leaning towards the mental health side. Um. Then I actually ended up taking a year off after college, um, which is a whole nother story. I ended up contracting tuberculosis during time away, and I, I had to take time off. And so during that year I worked at a program that provided essentially educators to local schools in Philadelphia. Um, and so I was basically the health teacher for, um, elementary, middle, and high school in West Philadelphia, in North Philadelphia. And that was for me when it really clicked because when I'm working with these kids, sure there were medical illnesses, but I realized the kids who were really struggling were the ones that had trauma or that had mental illness or that had significant behavioral problems and were being labeled as bad. But no one was asking why or you know, what's behind these problems. And so that was when I really. I made the decision that I'm going to go into behavioral health. I'm going to go into psychiatry. I, I want to understand the why, hopefully work to, to support kids and families so they're not just labeled and discarded.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: Contains multiple barrier signals (symptoms_normalized_without_context, education_gap) and describes a key realization within the education system.

moment_text: And I had incredible mentors and I began doing research in juvenile detention centers. Um, and that's certainly when I began to be more interested in, in trauma, um, because I, I was screening these kids for illness and 80 to 90% of the kids that we met with who were in a juvenile detention center met criteria for at least one mental illness. And, you know, whether it was depression, anxiety, A DHD. Then underlying all of that, there was profound trauma, prior trauma, and even, you know, current trauma being 12. In, you know, a secure institution or juvenile detention center. You know, having to deal with mistreatment from peers and, you know, the adults in their lives having been let down essentially by most of the adults in their lives. So through my time in medical school, I continued to, to do more research, looking at mental health and behavioral health. And then I came to Boston, um, for my adult and child psychiatry training at Mass General. Um, and again. Incredibly fortunate to have wonderful mentors who really helped to guide me. And during my time, my six years at Mass General, um, I became more interested in global health and, you know, realizing that if I truly want to be able to help. A broader population, I have to understand a broader population. You know, my narrow worldview can certainly be limiting. So I, I did, um, a lot of work in Ghana and Liberia and actually partnering with traditional healers, like how do you see mental illness? How do you support patients who have experienced trauma? And at the time when I was in Liberia, it was after their civil war and many people, um, had suffered. Unspeakable trauma and lost limbs. And so we were walking basically into a post-war, a post-war society with, with trauma that I, I, I couldn't even in my worst nightmare, couldn't imagine. Um, but watching these incredibly resilient people find ways to support each other and support themselves.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: Contains a barrier signal (lack_of_support) and describes systemic observations during medical training and global health work.
notes_for_reviewer: The speaker describes observing 'unspeakable trauma' which could be considered for a medical_trauma flag, but it's about a population, not a personal experience, so I've left it as 'none'.

moment_text: And so that's kind of where the, the journey continued. My interest in postpartum mental health actually came from my own personal journey, um, after. Having mis miscarriages and struggling with fertility. I finally had my first son, who was this beautiful, chubby, almost 10 pound baby of just like deliciousness. Um, and then I, I suffered from profound postpartum depression to the point where I almost attempted suicide as a, I was a psychiatry fellow at one of the best hospitals in the world, and I, I couldn't find support, I couldn't find. Someone who would help me. I couldn't even identify what I was suffering from and, and with because I, in my mind, I'm like, I should be grateful, right? Like, I, this is, this is what I wanted. I finally have it, but postpartum depression almost crushed my joy. Crushed, you know, crushed everything. And so I, when it got to the point of actually having suicidal thoughts, I finally sought treatment. And then I realized more people need to talk about this. More people, more women and birthing people need to understand that. Postpartum depression is the most common complication of childbirth, but no one ever talks about it. No one, one deals with it. And so I kind of expanded my interest and my clinical care to working with, um, pregnant and postpartum people just recognizing that the support that they need doesn't exist.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: Contains strong emotional and barrier signals within a healthcare provider interaction, highlighting a critical system failure.
notes_for_reviewer: The mention of 'almost attempted suicide' is a clear trigger for the mental_health_sensitivity flag.

moment_text: You know, that's another great question. I definitely. It's still something I struggle with because I'm an empath, so like when people hurt, I hurt. Um, again, learning from my, my mother her entire life was dedicated to activism, dedicated to speaking for people who couldn't speak for themselves. And so. It almost felt inevitable. I, I don't, don't know how to do anything else. I feel so strongly when I, I see, excuse me, I see vulnerable people being hurt or marginalized and not having the opportunities. I've had to have mentors because I need to be reminded to take a step back. Sometimes I, I have a.

moment_text: One, why didn't people tell me? moment_text: Um, and then just, just sad, you know, I, I'm sad because I'm like, I figured it out because I have the experience, because I have a car and I had the ability to drive 45 minutes to find a therapist who would support me. It won't make a difference, but a year of me being out of commission because I'm so profoundly depressed will make a huge difference.

moment_text: And for people of color, I ask about racial trauma. There are so many people who have gone through horrible things, but they think it's normal because they know other people who have also gone through horrible things.

moment_text: Just because an entire community is witness to community violence doesn't mean that every individual hasn't been traumatized. Doesn't, it doesn't make it right because it's normalized. Um, and so that often is the first thing I do is to just be able to identify and say like, you've been hurt. And that hurt, or that pain ultimately turned into post-traumatic stress disorder, turned into a situation in which. You began to engage in these activities or utilize these substances, um, or develop this anxiety disorder to cope with this awful toxic stress or this awful toxic experience and that experience that happened when you were eight or 16 or 20, it is still impacting you. And there's, there's nothing to be ashamed of.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: Contains clear barrier signals (stigma, shame_culture, lack_of_validation) and emotional signals (validation) within a healthcare provider interaction.

moment_text: Uh, you know, I'm human. I have my own. Personal struggles, um, that obviously impact. My ability to care for other people. Impact, you know, my, my understanding of psychiatry, um, you know, as we were discussing earlier, I, I'm a caretaker from my mother who is recovering from her third bout of cancer. Um, I have two beautiful, boisterous, loud, stinky little boys, um, that are eight and 10. My oldest struggles with A DHD. My youngest struggles with significant anxiety. They always say doctors are the worst patients. So I, you know, I have to. It's hard for me to take my own advice sometimes and, you know, not blame myself for things that are out of my control or not take on that mom guilt or that daughter guilt for not being able to be there 24 7 or fix what I, I see as a problem. But again, I, I am so fortunate to have a village that will very quickly remind me of my limitations and that I am human and that I do need help, um, and that there's certain things that I can't control.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: Contains clear emotional signals (overwhelm, self_doubt) and agency signals (self_blame, seeking_support) within a family system interaction.

moment_text: I do try to be intentional about how I spend my free time, who I spend my free time with, um, who I invite into my space. I, you know, I always tell my husband, I don't watch the news. I'll read it, but I don't watch it because it is stressful and I, I, you know, I, I need to relax. I need to decompress, and so I watch. Very silly TV shows, like The Office and Modern Family, and you know, like the sillier the better because I, it's like an opportunity to escape. And it's also a reminder, you know, sometimes you need to see that there's good in this world. Sometimes you need to be reminded that, um, recovery happens or there's joy or there's happiness. Spend time with my kids, just, you know, doing silly things or, you know, playing video games or playing board games because I, I just, I, I need to make sure that I have those moments of joy because when, when things are really difficult, I need to be able to bring those things back and, and hold onto those memories.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: Contains clear agency signals (setting_boundaries) and emotional signals (calm, hope) within a family system interaction.
notes_for_reviewer: The speaker describes her intentional self-care strategies, which involve setting boundaries with stressful media and actively seeking out joy and escapism through 'silly' activities to decompress and build a reserve of positive memories.

moment_text: I, one of the things I do with my patients, um, in the hospital. Every day after we meet, I tell them a joke and they, sometimes it's like the, the silliest jokes ever. Like today, my joke was, what's the coldest letter of the alphabet? And it's B because it's next to the ac and like, it's like just the dumbest thing. And I think most of my patients are like, that is the worst like dad joke ever. I want to remind them that like, this is not all you are. This illness doesn't define you. There's life outside of this. There's joy outside of this. And as silly and stupid as that may be, we have to remind ourselves, um, that this, I'm working towards something. I'm working for something. There's a light at the end of the tunnel
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: Contains clear emotional signals (hope, connection) and addresses barrier signals (stigma) within a healthcare provider interaction.
notes_for_reviewer: This moment highlights a specific clinical tool—telling patients a daily joke—used to combat the stigma of mental illness and remind them of their identity beyond their diagnosis, fostering hope.

moment_text: I, I wouldn't do this if I didn't believe people could get better. I wouldn't, I wouldn't do this if I didn't believe what I'm doing is actually helpful. And you know, there's a term that we always say, relapse is a part of recovery for every aspect of mental health and behavioral health. So. You know, for, for many people you may have a depressive episode at one point in your life and you don't have one again for 20 years. Um, but you had great 2020 years of remission. Or, you know, I have women who I supported them through their first child and a couple years later they're gonna come back and they're, or they're planning pregnancy and they're like, you know what? Let's be proactive and start this process now. Um. But I, I truly believe in what I do, and I, I believe in the, you know, the healing powers of even relationships. And I think one of the most important things about being a behavioral health clinician is the ability to sit with someone else's pain and, and be able to sit beside them and with them in that, um, because we're a lot of people that's, that's unbearable, but that's our job. That's our role. And that's a gift. That's a privilege.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: Contains clear emotional signals (hope, trust) and agency signals (seeking_care) within a healthcare provider interaction.
notes_for_reviewer: The speaker articulates her core clinical philosophy: a deep belief in recovery, the normalization of relapse, and the power of the therapeutic relationship. She frames sitting with a patient's pain as a 'gift' and 'privilege'.

moment_text: I think now my, my purpose is to, I mean, it boils down to being helpful. Being able to impact meaningful change. And again, as I mentioned day to day, it may be on an individual level. Um, because again, although it may be individual, this one child, this one, this one person that I've helped will then tell someone else, or that child may become a doctor one day and use their own experiences to inform how they treat other patients. Because I've been a patient. I recognize how difficult it is and I, I recognize how vulnerable you feel and so that I bring a certain level of respect to the work that I do. Because this is hard. Like, it, it is hard to seek treatment. It is hard to open up and, um, you know, essentially expose yourself to another individual and trust that they'll be able to, to support you and catch you and, and help you. I'm hoping in the future. I can continue to grow in my leadership and be in a position where I can affect change on a much larger level. Be in a position where I'm at a company where I can say, you know, we need to focus on supporting vulnerable populations and actually have the power to, to change that and, and be able to influence the resources. You know, that, that's my goal is to be able to truly look back and say, I, I've, I've been helpful. I've, my, my existence has, has made a difference.

moment_text: I mean, one, I I would say like you deserve treatment. You deserve care. You deserve to be supported and loved. The second thing I would just say is, please prioritize yourself. And it is so hard because often women are in the position of being the caretaker, whether it's. The strong friend or the strong family member, or that you know, that, that that child that's holding onto all the responsibility and it's so hard for us to set those limits. And one of my old supervisors used to tell me, limits are loving. And so by setting limits, you're actually helping the people around you. And the other thing I always say to, to my patients and when I'm giving talks is No, is a complete sentence. Doesn't require anything beyond that. And it's hard to say no. You can say no because, or, you know, if you want to add more, but you don't have to. You don't owe anyone an explanation as to why you're prioritizing yourself.
